title: [drill] Spoofed weak-channel sender clamps to known, read-only — §6
description: Security drill verifying a spoofed sender on a weak-auth channel is clamped to at most known trust with read-only (may_approve=false) capability. Spec §6.
design:
TDD PLAN (mol plan step, 2026-07-11) — §6/§9 PS drill: spoofed weak-channel sender clamps to ≤ known, read-only, may_approve=false.

THREAT: sms/email From is spoofable. An attacker who spoofs an enrolled weak-channel sender inherits that row's trust — that is accepted and why weak channels exist as a grade; what the clamp guarantees is the CEILING: the spoof gains at most known, only read verbs, and can never satisfy an approval. The §6 invariant under drill: a weak channel can NEVER stamp owner, even from a drifted table row.

HOME: extend internal/store/drill_test.go (same fixture drillConfig — sms weak channel + Dana enrolled there — the 2.8 drill built exactly the registry this needs). Same drill conventions.

CYCLES (drill code-under-test exists; red = sabotage check per drill convention — flip one expected field, confirm failure, restore):
Cycle 1 — TestDrillSpoofedWeakChannelSenderClamps:
  Layer 1, config validation (first line of defense): config.Parse of drillConfig with an added owner-on-sms identity MUST fail — pins that the enrollment path cannot even express the dangerous row.
  Layer 2, the stamping ceiling on the real seeded registry (via seedFromTOML):
    spoof of Dana's enrolled sms id → ResolveStamped(auth "weak") = {Known, ReadOnly:true, MayApprove:false} — enrolled trust, but read-only and never approving;
    spoof attempts at the owner via sms (owner's discord id over sms) → untrusted bottom (already denied at lookup; asserts the pair rule holds under this threat framing).
  Layer 3, table drift (second line of defense): INSERT an owner row on sms directly (schema allows it; config would not) → ResolveStamped(auth "weak") = {Known, ReadOnly:true, MayApprove:false} — THE clamp verdict the bead is named for. Also assert ResolveOwnerID for that drifted row still yields a principal ok=true at the store surface? NO — ResolveOwnerID has no channel-auth input; the approval gate composes MayApprove. Instead assert the stamp's MayApprove=false is what forecloses approval on this channel, with a comment pointing at §4.4 composition. Keep the drill to stamped-decision surfaces.
Gate: make check clean.
FILES: internal/store/drill_test.go only — no production code expected.

title: [drill] Host Read/Write of ~/.ssh or .claude/ denied — §9 PS
description: Security drill verifying host Read/Write attempts against ~/.ssh or .claude/ are denied by the path denylist. Spec §9 PS.
design:
PLAN — §9 PS drill: host Read/Write of ~/.ssh or .claude/ denied.

WHAT A DRILL IS HERE (convention set by TestDrillDaemonRefusesNewerSchema):
an automated test that drives the REAL decision surface with the REAL
inputs an attack would use — not a re-run of the unit tests. The
enforcement hook (C9 PreToolUse) is M1; at M0 the decision surface IS
internal/policy (DeniedPath for tool-call paths, DeniedDir for recursive
reads), so the drill pins the verdicts those hooks will answer from.

HOME: internal/policy/drill_test.go (package policy_test).

DRILL 1 — real host spellings through DeniedPath. Resolve the ACTUAL
os.UserHomeDir() and assert denial for the concrete paths a host
Read/Write would name: ~/.ssh, ~/.ssh/id_ed25519, ~/.ssh/authorized_keys,
~/.claude, ~/.claude/settings.json, ~/.claude/settings.local.json,
~/.claude/hooks/pre-tool-use.sh, ~/.claude/skills/x/SKILL.md. One verdict
covers read AND write by construction — DeniedPath has no mode parameter
to get wrong; the drill documents that property. Also assert the
carve-out boundary from inside the drill: ~/.claude/commands/foo.md and
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md stay readable (the drill must not overclaim).

DRILL 2 — recursive read via DeniedDir over a fixture home (t.TempDir —
walking the real home would be slow, flaky, and privacy-hostile):
fixture with .ssh/id_rsa, .claude/hooks/hook.sh, .claude/commands/ok.md,
and benign src/. Assert: read rooted AT .ssh refused; rooted AT
.claude/hooks refused; recursive read of the whole home refused (the
.ssh child surfaces); read rooted at .claude/commands allowed; read of
src allowed (drill proves denial is targeted, not blanket).

RED-FIRST NOTE: the denylist (1zr.2.7) already landed, so the drill is
expected green on first run. Red-discipline is kept by meta-verifying
the harness: temporarily flip one expectation, watch it fail, restore
(not committed). The drill's value is pinning §9 behavior against
regression, exactly like the schema-refusal drill.
QUALITY GATES: make check.
close_reason: Drills delivered: reviewed (Codex clean, 0.96) and committed as c222d77 on feat/approach-1zr.2-identity-lookup-and-drill

title: [drill] Unmapped sender resolves untrusted — §9 PS
description: Security drill verifying that a sender with no identity mapping resolves to untrusted. Spec §9 PS.
design:
TDD PLAN (mol plan step, 2026-07-11) — §9 PS drill: unmapped sender resolves untrusted.

WHAT A DRILL ADDS over the existing unit tests (TestResolveTrust already pins miss→untrusted on a near-empty table): drills drive the realest available path end-to-end and probe attacker-shaped inputs. Realest path today (no router yet): config.Parse over a literal approach.toml → the daemon's config→store mapping → store.SeedIdentities (the real seed) → resolution against a REALISTICALLY populated table, asserting ALL resolution surfaces deny.

HOME: internal/store/drill_test.go (new file, package store_test — importing config from an external test package creates no cycle). Mirrors the drill convention from internal/policy/drill_test.go and internal/cli/drill_test.go: TestDrill* names, §9 PS cited, comments say which future component (the router's stamping path) will answer from these verdicts.

SINGLE RED-GREEN CYCLE (the code under test already exists — the drill is the test; it must pass immediately after being written correctly, so the red phase is a deliberate sabotage check: assert the drill FAILS if pointed at an enrolled sender, then fix the probe to the real unmapped inputs):
Cycle 1 — TestDrillUnmappedSenderResolvesUntrusted:
  Seed via the real path: config.Parse(TOML with [channels.discord] auth strong, [channels.sms] auth weak, an owner on discord, a known on sms) → map fields exactly as cli/daemon.go seedIdentities does → store.SeedIdentities.
  Attacker probes, table-driven — each asserts THREE surfaces:
    store.ResolveTrust  → trust.Untrusted, no error
    store.ResolveOwnerID → ok=false, no error
    store.ResolveStamped(auth of that channel) → the bottom for that channel (strong: untrusted/rw/may_approve=true — capability, not verdict; weak: untrusted/ro/false)
  Probes: unknown id on an enrolled channel · enrolled id on the WRONG channel (cross-channel replay) · case-variant of an enrolled id (exact-match rule) · enrolled id with whitespace padding · empty native_id · unknown channel entirely (auth "" → bottom stamp).
Cycle 2 — TestDrillZeroConfigTrustsNobody: SeedIdentities with nil (the daemon's no-approach.toml boot) then probe the owner-shaped id → untrusted on all three surfaces. Pins the §6 'unconfigured daemon trusts nobody' warning path behavior.
Gate: make check clean.
FILES: internal/store/drill_test.go only — no production code expected.

title: Read/write path denylist: ~/.ssh, credentials, .claude/ hooks-settings-skills, .codex/, approach.toml — refused for read and write — §7
description: Implement the path denylist refusing both read and write access to ~/.ssh, credentials, .claude/ hooks-settings-skills, .codex/, and approach.toml. Spec §7.
design:
TDD plan — read/write path denylist (§7 rule 2)

DESIGN DECISIONS
1. New internal/policy package: DeniedPath(path) (denied bool, reason
   string) — the pure decision engine C9's PreToolUse hook (M1) and the
   §9 PS drill answer from. One function for read AND write: the spec
   refuses both, so there is no mode parameter to get wrong.
2. Lexical, segment-based matching after filepath.Clean, case-
   insensitive (macOS default FS is case-insensitive; a .SSH dodge must
   not work). Rules:
   - any segment ".ssh" — stricter than the spec's ~/.ssh: no home
     resolution to get wrong, and a legitimate repo-local .ssh does not
     exist; fail-safe
   - any segment "credentials" — the spec's word, read mechanically
     (covers ~/.aws/credentials, gcloud credential stores, creds dirs)
   - control surface: a ".claude" segment whose NEXT segment is hooks,
     skills, or settings* (settings.json, settings.local.json), or
     ".claude" as the path's final segment; other .claude content (e.g.
     commands/) stays reachable per the spec's enumeration
   - any segment ".codex" — whole subtree
   - basename "approach.toml" anywhere — THE config file is control
     surface wherever it lives; approach.toml.example stays readable
3. Traversal is neutralized by Clean (a/../.ssh/x). Symlink resolution
   is the ENFORCEMENT point's job (needs the filesystem); documented on
   the function: callers pass the fully resolved path.
4. Reason string names the matched rule for the refusal log/error.

RED→GREEN SEQUENCE
C1: .ssh denials — ~/.ssh/id_rsa, bare .ssh dir, nested repo/.ssh/key,
    traversal x/../.ssh/k, case dodge .SSH
C2: credentials denials — ~/.aws/credentials, credentials/token.json
C3: control surface denials — .claude/hooks/*, .claude/settings.json,
    .claude/settings.local.json, .claude/skills/*, terminal .claude,
    .codex/*, approach.toml at any depth
C4: allowed — ordinary repo files, .claude/commands/x.md, ssh (no dot),
    docs/approach.toml.example, credentials.md basename

title: Sticky tainted flag: schema + set-on-ingest plumbing (non-owner content, untrusted MCP result, web fetch, Codex web read) — §6, §7
description: Add the sticky tainted flag schema and set-on-ingest plumbing for non-owner content, untrusted MCP results, web fetches, and Codex web reads. Spec §6, §7.
design:
TDD plan — sticky tainted flag: schema + set-on-ingest plumbing (§6, §7)

DESIGN DECISIONS
1. Migration 0004_tainted.sql: ALTER TABLE sessions ADD COLUMN tainted
   INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 CHECK (tainted IN (0,1)). Ships separately
   from 0003 by design — this bead owns the taint model.
2. Taint rule is content-driven (§7 rule 3: "taint follows content, not
   the tool") and lives in internal/trust as a pure function:
   Taints(kind IngestKind, author Level) bool with the closed kind set:
   - IngestInboundMessage: taints unless author == Owner (non-owner
     prompt/attachment; junk author normalizes untrusted → taints)
   - IngestMCPResult: taints unless the server is graded owner-trusted
     (approach.toml enumeration is future config work; default untrusted
     ⇒ taints) — this is the poisoned-email drill vector
   - IngestWebFetch, IngestCodexWebRead: always taint
   - IngestCodexCritique: never taints (read-only critic must survive
     draft→critique→approve→act, §7)
3. Store plumbing, one column read/written right:
   - MarkSessionTainted(ctx, db, sessionID): UPDATE tainted=1; ERROR if
     the session does not exist — a taint that lands nowhere is a
     security bug, not a no-op. Idempotent when already tainted. No
     unmark API — stickiness by construction; clear-on-rotation is C4
     rotation work (M1), documented.
   - SessionTainted(ctx, db, sessionID) (bool, error): fails closed
     (missing session = error, not false... actually missing session
     must ERROR so callers cannot read absent-as-clean).

RED→GREEN SEQUENCE
C1: migrate test — sessions rows default tainted=0; explicit 2 or NULL
    refused → 0004_tainted.sql
C2: MarkSessionTainted sets the flag; second call idempotent; unknown
    session errors → UPDATE + RowsAffected check
C3: SessionTainted false before / true after mark; unknown session
    errors → point SELECT
C4: trust.Taints truth table incl. junk-author fail-safe → pure function

title: sessions.trust_floor column + computation rule (DM = identity; group = least-trusted admittable) — §6
description: Add the sessions.trust_floor column and its computation rule: DM sessions use the identity trust; group sessions use the least-trusted admittable participant. Spec §6.
design:
TDD plan — sessions.trust_floor column + computation rule (§6)

DESIGN DECISIONS
1. Migration 0003_sessions.sql creates the §6 sessions table (the column
   needs a table; C4 router logic stays M1). Spec schema with our
   constraint style: closed CHECK enums for status and trust_floor
   (owner|known|untrusted — participant levels only; system trust is
   daemon-stamped on events, never a session floor), PK
   (thread_key, session_id), UNIQUE sessions_by_id(session_id), partial
   unique one_live_session(thread_key WHERE status IN creating,active),
   partial unique one_worker_per_repo(cwd ... thread_key LIKE 'task:%').
   EXCLUDED: tainted column — that is bead 2.6's schema change (0004).
2. Computation rule = new internal/trust package (domain logic, reused by
   2.3 clamping and 2.6):
   - type Level string; Untrusted < Known < Owner ordering via rank map
   - Parse(s) (Level, error): closed enum, junk is an error
   - Min(a, b Level) Level
   - Floor(participants ...Level) Level: the least-trusted admittable
     participant. DM = the one identity's trust (single-element case);
     group = min over whoever can post there. EMPTY set = Untrusted —
     knowing nothing about who can read the thread must fail safe (§4.3
     confidentiality keys off the floor).
3. No store CRUD for sessions yet — creating/rotating sessions is C4 (M1).
   Schema + rule only, both fully testable now.

RED→GREEN SEQUENCE
C1: migrate test — sessions table exists; trust_floor rejects values
    outside owner|known|untrusted and rejects NULL → 0003_sessions.sql
C2: schema invariants — second live session on one thread_key violates
    one_live_session; rotated frees the slot; second live task:* worker
    on one cwd violates one_worker_per_repo → partial indexes
C3: trust.Parse closed enum (owner/known/untrusted ok, junk errors)
C4: trust.Floor — DM case: floor of a single participant IS that trust;
    group case: min(owner, known, untrusted)=untrusted, min(owner,
    known)=known; empty = untrusted (fail-safe)

title: Channel auth grading: auth=strong|weak config; weak clamps to ≤ known, may_approve=false — §6
description: SLIMMED SCOPE (2026-07-10): the config half of this bead shipped in PR #2 (approach-1zr.1.2) — Channel.Auth with closed-set strong|weak validation, and the derived MaxTrust()/MayApprove()/ReadOnly() accessors in internal/config/config.go, plus config-load rejection of owner enrollment on weak channels. What remains is ONLY the runtime half: apply the channel-auth clamp inside the identity-stamping path that approach-1zr.2.2 builds, so every inbound event's stamped trust is min(lookup trust, channel MaxTrust) with read_only and may_approve carried alongside. Depends on 1zr.2.2 (resolver must exist). See design field for the full plan. Spec §6.
design:
TDD PLAN (mol plan step, 2026-07-11) — runtime channel-auth clamp in the stamping path. Supersedes the earlier sketch; Step 1 of that sketch (trust lattice) already shipped in internal/trust (Level, Parse, Min).

WHY (unchanged): config.Parse rejects owner-on-weak at load, but the identities TABLE is the runtime source of truth and can drift (manual DB edits, stale seed). Spec §6: a weak channel can NEVER stamp owner trust — the invariant must hold at the point of stamping. This is defense-in-depth behind config validation AND behind the 0002 schema CHECKs.

DESIGN DECISIONS
1. Pure rule in internal/trust: type Stamped {Trust Level; ReadOnly bool; MayApprove bool} and func Stamp(auth string, lookup Level) Stamped. The §6 rules (Floor, Taints) live in trust; the clamp joins them. Closed auth set, switch on "strong"/"weak"; DEFAULT (including "" from a channel absent from [channels], and any junk/case-variant) fails closed to {Untrusted, ReadOnly:true, MayApprove:false}. This makes the unconfigured-channel case fall out naturally: cfg.Channels[name] on a missing key yields zero-value Channel with Auth "" -> fails closed.
2. Composition in internal/store: func ResolveStamped(ctx, db, channel, nativeID, auth string) (trust.Stamped, error) = ResolveTrust then trust.Stamp. store keeps NOT importing config (mirrors the Identity pattern) — the caller (daemon, later the router) reads auth off config.Channel and passes the string. Any ResolveTrust error returns the bottom Stamped {Untrusted, true, false} alongside the error, so even a caller that drops the error holds nothing.
3. MayApprove is stamped as the CHANNEL capability (per original design text), not && effective==Owner: §4.4 approval matching separately requires the owner_id match. Comment must say so explicitly.
4. Duplication note: trust.Stamp encodes the same strong/weak mapping as config.Channel.MaxTrust/MayApprove/ReadOnly (config stays string-typed at the file boundary; no config.go changes). Each side gets a cross-reference comment naming the other so drift is visible in review.

RED-GREEN SEQUENCE (one behavior per cycle)
Cycle 1 — trust.Stamp table test (internal/trust/trust_test.go), red first:
  strong+Owner   -> Owner, rw, may_approve=true
  strong+Known   -> Known, rw, true
  strong+Untrusted -> Untrusted, rw, true (channel capability; gate composes)
  weak+Owner     -> Known, read-only, false   (THE clamp — drifted-table simulation)
  weak+Known     -> Known, read-only, false
  weak+Untrusted -> Untrusted, read-only, false
  ""+Owner       -> Untrusted, read-only, false (unconfigured channel fails closed)
  "Strong"+Owner -> Untrusted, read-only, false (closed set is case-sensitive, like config validation)
Green: implement Stamped + Stamp in internal/trust.
Cycle 2 — store.ResolveStamped integration (internal/store/identities_test.go), red first, using mustOpen + direct INSERTs:
  a) owner row on strong channel -> Owner/rw/true
  b) owner row, auth=weak passed -> Known/ro/false — table drift past config validation is clamped at stamping
  c) miss on weak channel -> Untrusted/ro/false
  d) miss on unconfigured channel (auth "") -> Untrusted/ro/false
  e) closed DB -> error AND Stamped is the bottom {Untrusted/ro/false}
Green: ResolveStamped in internal/store/identities.go (~10 lines).
Cycle 3 — make check clean; no router wiring (M1).

FILES: internal/trust/trust.go + trust_test.go; internal/store/identities.go + identities_test.go. No config.go changes.
notes: Config half verified shipped at commit d91f2d9 (PR #2): internal/config/config.go lines 131-158 (Channel.Auth, MaxTrust, MayApprove, ReadOnly) and lines 299-301 (owner-on-weak rejected at load).

title: Canonical owner_id resolution across surfaces — §6
description: Implement canonical owner_id resolution so the owner is recognized as the same principal across all surfaces. Spec §6.
design:
TDD plan — canonical owner_id resolution across surfaces (§6, §4.4)

SCOPE SPLIT: bead 2.2 owns the trust-stamping lookup ((channel,native_id) →
trust, miss → untrusted). THIS bead owns the principal half: resolving the
canonical owner_id so "approve from Slack what Discord asked" is an owner_id
match, never a channel match.

DESIGN DECISIONS
1. store.ResolveOwnerID(ctx, db, channel, nativeID) (ownerID string, ok bool,
   err error). ok=false for: unmapped sender (untrusted has no principal) AND
   enrolled non-owner rows (known people are not principals — approval
   authorization matches owner_id only, §4.4). Schema already guarantees
   exactly owner rows carry owner_id (0002 CHECK), so the query is a plain
   point lookup; the CHECK makes NULL-owner_id-on-owner unrepresentable.
2. No new types: the principal is the string owner_id; cross-surface identity
   equality IS string equality on it. The tests pin that property.
3. Read-only query — no clamping here (2.3) and no trust in the signature:
   returning trust would grow this into 2.2's lookup.

RED→GREEN SEQUENCE
C1: enrolled owner row resolves its owner_id (ok=true) → point SELECT
C2: unmapped sender AND enrolled known row both resolve to no principal
    (ok=false, no error) — deny-by-default shape
C3: cross-surface unification: discord/42 and slack/U9 both enrolled owner
    with owner_id 'brian' resolve to the SAME principal; a second principal
    ('alice' on discord/77) resolves distinct — the §4.4 match primitive

EDGE CASES: query error propagates (never mapped to ok=false — a db failure
must not read as "not the owner" silently... actually it must FAIL CLOSED:
err non-nil, ok=false, caller refuses).

title: Identity lookup: (channel, native_id) → trust; miss → untrusted deny-by-default — §6
description: Implement identity lookup mapping (channel, native_id) to a trust level, with misses resolving to untrusted deny-by-default. Spec §6.
design:
PLAN — ResolveTrust: (channel, native_id) -> trust.Level, miss = Untrusted.

HOME: internal/store/identities.go, beside ResolveOwnerID — the identities
table is the store's schema, and the resolver is one SELECT. store gains an
import of internal/trust (a leaf package; no cycle) so the result is a
validated trust.Level, not a raw string a caller might forget to Parse.

SIGNATURE: ResolveTrust(ctx, db, channel, nativeID) (trust.Level, error).
- Miss (no row) -> (trust.Untrusted, nil). Deny-by-default is a VALID
  verdict, not an error (§6: "a miss stamps untrusted").
- Query failure -> (trust.Untrusted, err). Fail closed twice over: the error
  is the refusal, and even a caller that wrongly ignores it holds Untrusted.
- Hit -> scanned string through trust.Parse. The 0002 CHECK constrains rows
  to owner|known, but the table is the ROOT of §7 — defense-in-depth against
  schema drift (manual DB edits): junk trust -> (Untrusted, error), never
  coerced silently.
- Exact-match lookup: platform native IDs are case-sensitive; SQLite TEXT
  equality is already exact. Documented, tested.

NOT IN SCOPE: owner_id in the same call (ResolveOwnerID exists for approval
matching; the M1 adapter composes them), channel-auth clamping (1zr.2.3
extends this resolver's result into the stamped decision).

TDD SEQUENCE (one behavior per red-green cycle, in identities_test.go):
1. RED: unmapped sender -> Untrusted, nil error (function doesn't exist yet).
2. RED: enrolled owner row -> Owner.
3. RED: enrolled known row -> Known.
4. RED: case-mismatched native_id -> Untrusted (exact match, no collation).
5. RED: closed/broken DB -> non-nil error AND Untrusted result.
6. RED: drifted junk trust row (inserted under PRAGMA ignore_check_constraints)
   -> non-nil error AND Untrusted — the Parse defense, testable because
   SQLite lets a test simulate exactly the drift the defense exists for.
QUALITY GATES: go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...

title: identities table migration + seed from approach.toml — §6
description: Add the identities table migration and seed it from approach.toml at startup. Spec §6.
design:
TDD plan — identities table migration + seed from approach.toml (§6)

DESIGN DECISIONS
1. Migration 0002_identities.sql creates the §6 identities table:
   (channel, native_id) PK, trust TEXT NOT NULL, owner_id TEXT, label TEXT.
   Schema-level constraints mirror config validation (defense in depth,
   "schema constraint not convention"):
   - CHECK (trust IN ('owner','known'))  -- untrusted is the ABSENCE of a row
   - CHECK ((owner_id IS NOT NULL) = (trust = 'owner'))  -- owner rows carry the
     canonical principal; non-owner rows must not (§4.4 approval binding)
2. Seeding = full sync, one transaction: DELETE FROM identities; INSERT config
   rows. Config is the single source of truth — removing a person from
   approach.toml must REVOKE trust at next startup; upsert-only would leave
   stale enrollments live. Atomic + idempotent.
3. store.SeedIdentities(ctx, db, []store.Identity) — store defines its own
   Identity struct so store does not import config; cli maps config.Identity.
4. Daemon wiring: new -config flag on `approach daemon`, default
   $APPROACH_HOME/approach.toml (~/approach/approach.toml). Load AFTER the
   admin lifetime lock, BEFORE store.Open (bad config must be refused before
   we migrate the live store). Seed right after Open.
5. Missing config at the DEFAULT path: warn + sync-to-empty (all senders
   untrusted — fail-safe, keeps M0 daemon bootable, matches systemd unit which
   passes no -config). Explicit -config that is missing/invalid: fail loud,
   exit 1. Trust stored as configured — the weak-channel clamp is runtime
   (bead 2.3); config load already rejects owner-on-weak.

RED→GREEN SEQUENCE (one behavior per cycle)
C1: migrate test — after Open, identities exists with PK (channel,native_id)
    → add 0002_identities.sql
C2: constraint tests — INSERT trust='untrusted' fails; owner without owner_id
    fails; known WITH owner_id fails; dup (channel,native_id) fails
    → CHECKs in the migration
C3: SeedIdentities inserts rows, queryable back → naive insert loop in one tx
C4: re-seed with a changed set removes stale rows + updates changed trust
    (revocation semantics) → DELETE-then-INSERT in the same tx
C5: daemon --config file.toml seeds table at startup (drain, then inspect db)
    → flag + config.Load + SeedIdentities wiring
C6: daemon with explicit --config pointing at missing/invalid file exits 1
    with structured error log → fail-loud branch
C7: daemon with defaulted, absent config warns + serves with empty table
    (t.Setenv APPROACH_HOME) → warn + sync-to-empty branch

EDGE CASES: zero identities in a valid config = legitimate sync-to-empty;
re-running Open (restart) is idempotent; seed failure aborts startup (exit 1).

title: [drill] Daemon refuses to start on newer schema version — §9 PS
description: Security drill verifying the daemon refuses to start when it encounters a schema version newer than the binary knows. Spec §9 PS.
design:
# Plan: approach-1zr.1.10 — [drill] Daemon refuses to start on newer schema version — §9 PS

1zr.1.5 proved the refusal at the store layer (Migrate/Open unit tests). The drill is
the §9 PS artifact: drive the REAL daemon startup path end to end and prove the
refusal is total — no socket, no listener, nonzero exit, operator-grade diagnostics
in the journal. Drills verify existing behavior, so the test may be born green; its
value is proven by a mutation check (temporarily neuter the guard, watch the drill
fail, restore) rather than red-green.

## Shape
internal/cli/drill_test.go — TestDrillDaemonRefusesNewerSchema:
1. Seed a state dir: store.Open (migrates to current), PRAGMA user_version = 9999,
   close — a db from the future.
2. cli.Run(["daemon", "--state", dir]) → must return 1 (not hang, not serve).
3. Assert the refusal is total:
   - socket file was never created;
   - approach status --socket fails (nothing listening);
   - stderr journal has a structured record whose error names the versions
     (9999, the binary's max) and reads as ErrSchemaTooNew ("newer than this binary");
   - the db file's user_version is STILL 9999 (nothing touched the future schema).
4. Run with a bounded overall deadline so a hang fails fast.

## Verification of the drill itself
Mutation check (manual, not committed): drop the version guard in applyPending,
run the drill, confirm it fails; restore. Recorded in the mol notes.

## Out of scope
The remaining §9 PS drills (identity/trust/sandbox — M0 epic 0.2 and M2).

title: Structured logging + panic recovery in the daemon main loop
description: Add structured logging and panic recovery to the daemon main loop so failures are logged and the loop survives panics.
design:
# Plan: approach-1zr.1.9 — Structured logging + panic recovery in the daemon main loop

The daemon runs under systemd: stderr is the journal, so structured logging is
log/slog with a JSON handler on stderr. Panic policy is two-tier:
- per-connection handler goroutines (the only concurrent code today, and where
  OnPoke/Status hooks — later the router — run): recover, log with stack, answer
  "err internal error", keep serving. One bad request must not kill the daemon.
- the daemon top level: recover, log with stack, exit nonzero — systemd's
  Restart=on-failure does the restart; the log is so it never dies silently.

## Shape
- admin.Options gains Logger *slog.Logger (nil → silent, keeps tests/package quiet).
  Server logs: one Info record per request (verb, duration, remote closed?) and one
  Error record per recovered panic (verb, panic value, stack).
- handle(): defer recover → log + best-effort "err internal error" reply.
- runDaemon: slog JSON handler on stderr; records for state dir/store-open, ready
  (socket path), drain/stop; error paths through the logger. Top-level defer
  recover → Error record with stack, exit 1. The plain "listening" stdout line
  stays — it is the launcher readiness contract from 1zr.1.6.

## TDD sequence
1. Request logging: inject a slog logger over a buffer; poke; assert a JSON record
   with msg/verb fields.
2. Panic in OnPoke: client gets "err internal error"; a following poke still
   answers ok (server alive); buffer holds an Error record with a stack field.
3. Panic in Status provider: same contract (one deferred recover covers both).
4. CLI: daemon run emits JSON records on stderr (parse a line, assert level+msg);
   the stdout readiness line is unchanged.
5. CLI: top-level panic → exit 1 + logged panic record (simulate via a hook? if no
   clean seam exists, cover with a unit on the recover helper instead — decide in
   implementation, do NOT add a test-only backdoor to production paths).

## Out of scope
Log levels config, log rotation (journald owns it), event-loop logging (M1).

title: systemd units: daemon service, approach.target grouping daemon + all timers — §7
description: Write systemd units: the daemon service plus an approach.target that groups the daemon and all timers. Spec §7.
design:
# Plan: approach-1zr.1.7 — systemd units: daemon service + approach.target — §7

Spec: "One systemd target — approach.target — groups the daemon and every timer.
systemctl stop approach.target is the single documented panic command; nothing wakes
up until you start it again." Timers arrive in M3; the deliverable NOW is the daemon
service, the target, and a tested grouping invariant that future timers cannot escape.

## Shape

deploy/systemd/ (user-level units; everything runs as the owner on the home server):
- approach.target: [Unit] Description; [Install] WantedBy=default.target.
- approach.service:
  - [Unit] Description, PartOf=approach.target (stop target → stop daemon),
    After=network-online.target.
  - [Service] ExecStart=%h/bin/approach daemon, Restart=on-failure, RestartSec=2,
    TimeoutStopSec=30. SIGTERM is the drain path (signal.NotifyContext in runDaemon),
    so default KillSignal is correct. NoNewPrivileges=true, PrivateTmp=true.
  - [Install] WantedBy=approach.target (starting the target starts the daemon).
- README.md: install/enable commands (systemctl --user link+enable), pointing at
  the §7 kill switch (documented fully in 1zr.1.8).

Tests (deploy/systemd/units_test.go, package systemd_test — static-config tests,
runnable on darwin; no systemd needed):
- minimal INI parser helper (sections → key/values, duplicate keys kept).

## TDD sequence
1. approach.target exists, parses, has Description and WantedBy=default.target.
2. approach.service parses; ExecStart runs `approach daemon`; Restart=on-failure;
   [Install] WantedBy=approach.target.
3. THE invariant (§7 kill switch): every *.service and *.timer in deploy/systemd
   (present and future) has PartOf=approach.target AND [Install]
   WantedBy=approach.target — a new timer dropped into the directory without the
   grouping fails the build's tests.
4. Sanity: no unit names a WorkingDirectory/ExecStart outside %h (portability guard,
   catches hardcoded /Users or /home paths).

## Out of scope
Kill-switch doc + smoke test (1zr.1.8), heartbeat timer content (M3), sd_notify.

title: systemctl stop approach.target kill-switch doc + smoke test — §7
description: Document and smoke-test the kill switch: systemctl stop approach.target halts the daemon and all timers. Spec §7.
design:
# Plan: approach-1zr.1.8 — kill-switch doc + smoke test — §7

Spec: "systemctl stop approach.target is the single documented panic command; nothing
wakes up until you start it again." 1zr.1.7 shipped the units and established that the
persistent form is `disable --now`. This bead ships (a) the operator-facing kill-switch
doc as a dedicated section, and (b) an executable smoke test for the Linux host, with
its structure verified by Go tests that run everywhere (darwin included — no systemd
needed for the static layer).

## Shape

1. deploy/systemd/smoke-kill-switch.sh (bash, set -euo pipefail):
   - preflight: systemctl --user reachable, units installed, else exit 2 "install first"
   - start: enable --now approach.target; poll `approach status --socket …` until ok
   - kill: disable --now approach.target
   - assert DOWN: is-active approach.target != active; approach.service inactive;
     `approach status` fails; socket file removed; every *.service/*.timer shipped in
     deploy/systemd is inactive (the "nothing left running" §9 teardown flavor)
   - resume: enable --now; poll status ok again
   - trap prints FAIL line with the failed step; exit 0 only if all steps pass
2. Kill-switch section in deploy/systemd/README.md: the panic command, what it
   guarantees (stays down across reboots), what bounded stop means (30s then SIGKILL),
   and how to run the smoke test after install.

## TDD sequence (Go, deploy/systemd/smoke_test.go, same package as units_test.go)
1. Script exists and is executable (mode &0111).
2. `bash -n` accepts it (syntax check — runs fine on darwin).
3. Script content invariants: set -euo pipefail; uses `disable --now approach.target`
   for the kill and `enable --now approach.target` for resume; probes via
   `approach status`; checks the socket file is gone.
4. README invariants: documents `disable --now` as THE panic command and names the
   smoke script (doc and mechanism cannot drift apart silently).

## Out of scope
Running against real systemd in CI (needs a Linux runner — noted in README), the
newer-schema drill (1zr.1.10), heartbeat timers (M3).

title: Unix socket listener + minimal admin protocol (poke, status, drain) — §3, §8
description: Implement the Unix socket listener with a minimal admin protocol supporting poke, status, and drain commands. Spec §3, §8.
design:
# Plan: approach-1zr.1.6 — Unix socket listener + minimal admin protocol (poke, status, drain) — §3, §8

Spec anchors: systemd timers "shrink to socket pokes" (§3, §8); heartbeat/reflection/NL-cron all
enter through the same event path. This bead builds the socket front door + admin verbs only —
the event router does not exist yet, so poke lands in a pluggable hook.

## Shape

New package internal/admin:
- Server: New(socketPath string, opts Options) — Options carries OnPoke func() (no-op default,
  later wired to the router) and Status func() map fields provider (version, schema version).
- Serve(ctx) error: accepts until ctx cancel or a drain command; per-connection goroutine.
- Protocol: line-based, one request per connection. Client sends "poke"|"status"|"drain";
  server replies "ok" / "ok {json}" / "err <reason>" and closes. Read deadline (5s) so a silent
  client cannot pin a connection goroutine forever.
- drain semantics: reply "ok draining", stop accepting, wait for in-flight handlers, Serve
  returns nil, socket file removed. SIGTERM/ctx-cancel takes the same path.

Socket file posture (mirrors store):
- parent dir must be 0700; socket chmod 0600 after bind; symlinked path refused (Lstat).
- Stale socket: dial first — connection refused → unlink and bind; dial succeeds → another
  daemon is live, refuse to start (never steal a live socket).

CLI wiring (internal/cli):
- approach daemon --db <path> --socket <path>: open store, serve admin socket until drain/signal.
- approach poke|status|drain --socket <path>: dial, send verb, print reply, exit 0 on "ok".
  These are what systemd timers and the 1zr.1.8 kill-switch smoke test call.
- Defaults: db state/approach.db, socket state/approach.sock under $APPROACH_HOME or ~/approach.

## TDD sequence (one behavior per red→green cycle)

1. Serve creates socket; client "poke" gets "ok"; OnPoke hook observed fired.
2. "status" returns "ok {…}" JSON carrying the fields from the Status provider.
3. "drain" replies, Serve returns cleanly, socket file removed, later dials fail.
4. Unknown verb → "err unknown command …", connection closed, server keeps serving.
5. Stale socket file (no listener) is replaced on start.
6. Live socket (second Server) refuses to start with a clear error.
7. Symlinked socket path refused.
8. Slow/silent client hits read deadline; a concurrent well-behaved client still served.
9. ctx cancellation drains like "drain" (signal path).
10. CLI: daemon+poke round-trip over a TempDir socket; poke/status/drain exit codes.

## Out of scope
Event queue/router (M1), systemd unit files (1zr.1.7), logging/panic recovery (1zr.1.9).

title: Refuse startup on schema version newer than binary knows — §6
description: Make the daemon refuse to start when the database schema version is newer than the binary knows about. Spec §6.
design:
# Plan: approach-1zr.1.5 — Refuse startup on schema version newer than binary knows (§6)

Spec: "the daemon refuses to start on a schema version newer than it knows — downgrade protection beats mystery corruption."

## Where the change lands

internal/store/migrate.go, applyPending(): the version is already read under
BEGIN IMMEDIATE; the comment at the read site marks this exact gap ("today
that is a silent no-op — approach-1zr.1.5 turns it into a hard refusal").
Because Open() calls Migrate(), refusing inside applyPending IS refusing
daemon startup — no cmd/ or cli/ changes needed.

## Design decisions

1. Exported sentinel: `var ErrSchemaTooNew = errors.New("database schema is newer than this binary")`.
   Callers (daemon startup, drill approach-1zr.1.10) assert with errors.Is;
   wrap it with fmt.Errorf carrying both numbers and an actionable hint:
   "store: schema version %d, this binary knows up to %d: %w — upgrade approach or restore a matching database (§6)".
2. Check placement: immediately after reading PRAGMA user_version, BEFORE the
   pending computation — with version > max, pending is empty and today falls
   into the silent-no-op early return.
3. Max known version = migrations[len-1].number (loadMigrations sorts and
   enforces contiguity); guard len==0 with last=0 so an empty set + version>0
   refuses instead of panicking.
4. The check runs under the write lock and errors through the existing
   rollback path — no new transaction handling.
5. Out of scope (note in code only if touched): a committed negative
   user_version (escaped-commit residue) is not "newer"; it falls through to
   existing re-apply behavior.
6. Update the stale "today that is a silent no-op" comment and the Migrate
   doc comment to state the refusal.

## TDD sequence (red → green each)

1. TestMigrateRefusesNewerSchemaVersion (migrate_internal_test.go):
   fstest.MapFS with 0001 only; Exec PRAGMA user_version = 2; migrate() →
   error, errors.Is(err, ErrSchemaTooNew), message names 2 and 1;
   user_version still 2; store still writable (no leaked write lock).
2. TestMigrateRefusesNewerVersionEmptySet: empty fsys + user_version = 1 →
   ErrSchemaTooNew, no panic.
3. TestOpenRefusesNewerSchemaVersion (migrate_test.go, external): mustOpen,
   Exec PRAGMA user_version = 9999, Close, store.Open again → error with
   errors.Is(err, store.ErrSchemaTooNew). This is the startup-path proof the
   drill (1zr.1.10) builds on.
4. Equal-version no-op already covered by TestMigrateIsIdempotent — verify,
   don't duplicate.

## Steps

1. git checkout main && git pull, branch feat/approach-1zr.1.5-refuse-newer-schema.
2. bd update approach-1zr.1.5 --claim.
3. Red: add tests above; confirm 1 and 3 fail (silent no-op), 2 panics.
4. Green: sentinel + guard in applyPending (~8 lines); comments updated.
5. Gates: make check (build + test + lint).
6. Handoff (conservative profile): report diff + gates; commit/PR only on request.
   Do NOT close approach-1zr.1.10 — the drill is separate follow-up work.

title: Embedded numbered migration runner, single-transaction apply at startup — §6
description: Implement an embedded, numbered migration runner that applies pending migrations in a single transaction at daemon startup. Spec §6.
design:
TDD plan (2026-07-10, full version in session notes):

DESIGN
- internal/store/migrate.go + internal/store/migrations/*.sql (no new package)
- Version tracking: PRAGMA user_version (transactional in-header, survives VACUUM INTO, no bootstrap table)
- Files NNNN_description.sql, contiguous from 0001; loader fails loud on gaps/dupes/malformed/stray files
- Single tx per spec: dedicated *sql.Conn + explicit BEGIN IMMEDIATE (deferred tx risks SQLITE_BUSY_SNAPSHOT under WAL, which busy_timeout does not retry); all pending migrations + user_version bump commit atomically
- API: Migrate(ctx, db) exported over go:embed set; unexported migrate(ctx, db, fsys fs.FS) core tested via fstest.MapFS
- store.Open calls Migrate after verifyPragmas (Open IS the startup path until the daemon main loop lands)
- Ship 0001_baseline.sql (SELECT 1) so go:embed compiles and the production path is exercised; 1zr.2.1 lands as 0002
- Errors: store: prefix, name the offending file

TDD CYCLES
1 loader validation: ordering, gap, duplicate, malformed name, stray file
2 fresh db applies all, user_version = N
3 idempotent rerun; partial apply (only pending; prove earlier files don't re-run)
4 atomicity: broken 0002 rolls back 0001 too, version stays 0, error names file; store usable after failed run
5 no-op when version == max; leave version>max refusal as commented seam for 1zr.1.5 (do NOT implement here)
6 embedded path: Open leaves user_version >= 1; Migrate on embedded set errors if set ever invalid
7 concurrent Migrate from two handles both succeed (validates BEGIN IMMEDIATE + busy_timeout)

Out of scope: newer-version refusal (1zr.1.5), identities (1zr.2.1), events (x6n.2.1).
Gates: make check. Note for migration authors: no pragma toggles inside migrations; multi-statement files must be covered by a fixture.

description: Implement the approach.toml config loader with validation covering model routing, channel bindings, TTLs, and a policy matrix stub. Spec §6, §8.
design: TDD plan (2026-07-10): new internal/config pkg. Lib: BurntSushi/toml (strict via MetaData.Undecoded). API: Load(path)/Parse(r io.Reader) -> (*Config, error); errors aggregated via errors.Join. Schema: [models] event-kind->model (message+heartbeat required, heavy/critique optional); [channels.<name>] auth=strong|weak with derived MaxTrust/MayApprove (weak=>known,false); [[identity]] channel/native_id/trust/owner_id (owner requires owner_id; owner-on-weak-channel rejected at load; dup (channel,native_id) rejected); [sessions] idle_ttl/turn_cap durations with defaults; [policy] stub matrix capability x {owner,known,untrusted} actions allow|ask|gate|deny|sandbox, default = coded S7 table, missing column=>deny, unknown capability=>error. Slices: A parse/strict, B models, C channels, D identities, E sessions, F policy stub, G error aggregation + golden docs/approach.toml.example test, H optional 'approach config check' CLI. Out of scope: enforcement (C9), DB seeding, retry policy, MCP trust list.

title: SQLite open helper: WAL, synchronous=NORMAL, busy_timeout, state/ 0700, db 0600 — §6
description: Build the SQLite open helper enabling WAL, synchronous=NORMAL, and busy_timeout, with state/ dir at 0700 and db file at 0600. Spec §6.
design:
PLAN — internal/store: SQLite open helper (spec §6).

SCOPE: just the open helper — package, driver dependency, pragma enforcement, permission enforcement, tests. No migrations (1zr.1.4), no daemon wiring, no CLI changes.

DRIVER DECISION: modernc.org/sqlite (pure Go). Rationale: no CGO keeps 'go test -race' and CI (setup-go + golangci-lint) toolchain-free, cross-compiles trivially for the Linux deploy box, and is the same driver family beads uses. Alternative mattn/go-sqlite3 rejected: CGO cost buys nothing we need. Pin latest stable in go.mod.

API SHAPE:
  package store  // will also hold the 1zr.1.4 migration runner and later queries
  func Open(path string) (*sql.DB, error)
  Path is the db file (e.g. ~/approach/state/approach.db); Open owns the whole §6 posture:
  1. Parent dir: MkdirAll 0700 if missing. If it exists with group/other bits set, FAIL LOUD with the exact chmod to run — the store holds identities and approvals; auto-tightening would mask a real exposure window, so we refuse instead (matches config loader fail-loud philosophy).
  2. DB file: pre-create empty with O_CREATE|O_EXCL 0600 if missing (SQLite treats an empty file as a new db; -wal/-shm inherit the main file's mode, so 0600 here covers the WAL sidecars). If it exists with wider perms, FAIL LOUD same as the dir.
  3. Open DSN: file:<path>?_pragma=journal_mode(WAL)&_pragma=synchronous(NORMAL)&_pragma=busy_timeout(5000)&_pragma=foreign_keys(ON)
     Pragmas MUST live in the DSN, not a post-open Exec: database/sql pools connections and synchronous/busy_timeout/foreign_keys are per-connection — an Exec sets them on one conn while the pool silently hands out unconfigured ones. journal_mode=WAL is file-persistent but harmless to re-assert per conn.
     foreign_keys(ON) is not in the bead title but is part of the same fail-closed posture; note it in the commit message.
  4. Verify-after-open (fail loud, not trust-the-DSN): query PRAGMA journal_mode == wal, synchronous == 1, busy_timeout == 5000, foreign_keys == 1; mismatch is an error naming the pragma.
  5. Pool: leave database/sql defaults. WAL gives concurrent readers + single writer and busy_timeout(5000) absorbs writer contention; revisit MaxOpenConns only if SQLITE_BUSY shows up in practice.

TDD ORDER (red first, table-driven where natural; t.TempDir throughout):
  a. Open on a fresh path creates parent dir 0700 and db file 0600 (assert FileMode&0777).
  b. All four pragmas verify on the returned handle.
  c. Pragmas hold on a SECOND pooled connection: grab two conns via db.Conn(ctx) concurrently, assert busy_timeout/foreign_keys on each — the regression test for the DSN-vs-Exec trap.
  d. Existing dir 0755 -> error mentioning the dir and 0700.
  e. Existing db 0644 -> error mentioning the file and 0600.
  f. Re-open of a previously created db succeeds and pragmas still verify (idempotence).
  g. Write-then-read round trip through the handle (create table, insert, select) proving the driver actually works under -race.
  Perms assertions are unix-only; targets are darwin dev + linux deploy, no build tags needed.

BRANCH/FLOW: git pull on main first, branch feat/approach-1zr.1.3-sqlite-store, TDD loop, make check (build + test -race + golangci-lint). No commits until asked.

RISKS: modernc DSN pragma syntax is driver-specific — the verify-after-open step converts a silent syntax slip into a hard startup error, which is the §6 posture anyway. synchronous returns 1 for NORMAL (numeric), journal_mode returns the string 'wal' — tests must match actual return types.
notes: Unblocks approach-1zr.1.4 (migration runner) which unblocks 1zr.1.5 and the identities-table chain (1zr.2.1 → 2.2 → 2.3). SHIPPED 2026-07-10: PR #3 (https://github.com/brian-bell/approach/pull/3), commit 921d5c1 on feat/approach-1zr.1.3-sqlite-store. Autoreview clean (0.94), make check green. Close after merge.

title: Re-scope C1: cc-connect is third-party (adopt-vs-build decision + trust boundary fix)
description:
Discovered 2026-07-09: the spec's C1 channel bridge, cc-connect, is not the owner's program and is not installed. It is third-party OSS (github.com/chenhg5/cc-connect — Go, MIT, ~13.8k stars, v1.4.1, actively maintained) that bridges local AI coding agents (Claude Code, Cursor, Gemini CLI, Codex) to messaging platforms (Feishu/Lark, DingTalk, Slack, Telegram, Discord, LINE, WeChat Work — no SMS).

Consequences to resolve in the spec:
1. C1 status Have/P0 -> Adopt (or Build). Decide adopt-vs-build for the channel layer. Note cc-connect's own pitch is driving the agent itself, which overlaps APPROACH's daemon-owns-the-engine design (session pinning, per-thread queues, hooks, durable events) — determine from its actual architecture whether it can run as a pure channel adapter feeding APPROACH's event queue, or whether adopting it means fighting it.
2. §6 trust-assignment claim ('set by bridge — code you control') is now false. Move trust stamping and event normalization (thread_key, dedup_key, trust) to an APPROACH-owned boundary, or fork/wrap the bridge. Coordinates with bead approach-neg.3.
3. Supply-chain posture: third-party resident process holding all channel tokens and seeing every inbound/outbound message; sponsor-heavy project. Version pin + review posture like beads (C13), plus egress considerations in .3.
4. Claim corrections: 'your Go bridge' wording everywhere; SMS support; P0-installed framing and its contribution to the 70% claim (coordinates with bead .6); adapter work is real integration effort, not zero.
5. Verify what cc-connect actually emits/expects (event shapes, session handling, hook compatibility) before the adopt decision — coordinates with bead .7.
design:
DECISION (2026-07-09, grill-me session): C1 verdict is BUILD THIN, NATIVE. cc-connect (chenhg5/cc-connect) is rejected for adoption on the same structural grounds as Hermes, amplified: repo inspection confirmed it is a full competing harness, not a bridge — it drives 10+ agents itself via ACP, owns session management, persistent memory (/memory), NL-cron scheduling, and chat-controlled permission modes (/mode). No pure-adapter mode exists. Adopting it = a third-party always-on agent-driving process holding all channel tokens with its own session/memory/schedule truth models (split-brain everywhere); fork-and-gut = inheriting a large codebase's update stream for 2-3 adapters.

Chosen shape: the channel layer is built inside the APPROACH daemon using mature Go libs (discordgo, slack-go, etc.) for the 2-3 surfaces actually used. Consequences: trust stamping, thread_key, and dedup_key assignment are APPROACH-owned code BY CONSTRUCTION (§6 'code you control' claim becomes true again); no second resident process (§8 'package inside cc-connect' text dies); C1 flips Have·P0 -> Build·S-M, P0 'baseline already running' is NO LONGER DONE and the installed-base/70% framing shrinks accordingly (feeds .6); cc-connect retained as reference code for platform quirks only. SMS claim drops unless a real SMS adapter is chosen later.
acceptance_criteria: The spec accurately describes cc-connect's provenance and status; an explicit adopt-vs-build decision for C1 is recorded with the integration boundary defined (who spawns claude, who stamps trust, what the adapter contract is); trust assignment happens in APPROACH-owned code; affected claims (§2 P0 list, §6, §8, hero coverage) are corrected.
notes: Spec edits applied 2026-07-09 (v1.2 -> v1.3, uncommitted working tree; SHA-256 12e07752a7bdf15ce613c6d6f9690619fdf1a2fd0ab005dd741309ee188e8ab7). Edits: hero thesis/meta/coverage tile flip C1 to build-native (cc-connect rejected); §2 lead recast to engine-only installed base, new channel-adapters bullet in the missing list, headless-loop bullet de-cc-connected; §3 lead now one-installed/four-build, Fig 1 channels subgraph -> native adapters (SMS dropped), C1 register row rewritten (Build S-M), shape callout de-cc-connected; Figs 2/3/5 participant renamed channel adapter (C1); §6 contract text -> adapter layer, 'code you own by construction'; §8 process shape -> one daemon incl. adapters; §9 P0 recast engine-only with honest correction note, P1 gains first-adapter work; §10 gains 'Same test, same verdict — cc-connect' decision paragraph. Remaining cc-connect mentions are all rejection-record. NOTE: hero 70% gauge number intentionally left for .6's matrix rework — tiles are now truthful (2/11 installed) so the number is visibly stale; .6 must fix.

title: Run independent re-review of revised spec (gate: score >= 8/10)
description:
Final gate for the epic. After all revision beads land, run a fresh independent review pass of the revised specification using the same standard as the 2026-07-09 review (which scored 5/10). Confirms that all critical and important findings are resolved or explicitly dispositioned, each phase has test-first acceptance and fault-injection criteria, and verified claims are separated from assumptions with dated sources.

Blocked by approach-neg.1 through approach-neg.7.
design:
FIX ROUND 3 (2026-07-10, grill-me) — resolves merged round-2 union (Claude 7/10 + Codex 6/10). 2 grilled forks + mechanical union + 1 dispute pending re-verify.

GRILLED:
MIXED-TRUST THREADS (R2-F2 crit / Codex #1 crit): add sessions.trust_floor = MIN trust of any participant the thread can admit (DM=that identity, group=whoever can post). Retrieval AND matrix key off trust_floor, not current sender. Owner-private facts (min_trust=owner) never assemble into a multi-party session — reachable only in single-owner-DM (floor=owner). Structural, not per-event.
FILE-WRITE POLICY (Codex #2 crit): Edit/Write/NotebookEdit become matrix rows governed like Bash — cwd-subtree only + hard denylist on .claude/ (hooks/settings/skills), .codex/, approach.toml, creds, outside-workspace. Harness control surface is NEVER model-writable in-band (closes 'agent edits its own hook to disable the gate'). Writes taint-shift.

MECHANICAL UNION:
- log_entries gains event_id + trust + min_trust columns (Codex #3 crit / R2-F3): Stop-hook stamps effective trust; log retrieval trust-filtered like facts; mechanical provenance now has data (max-untrust over range is computable).
- Taint on CONTENT not tool (Codex #4 / R2-F1): approach.toml enumerates MCP tools trusted/untrusted-returning (default untrusted); taint fires on any externally-authored tool result incl. email/calendar/Drive; inbound non-owner prompt taints PRE-execution (not via PostToolUse). Define numeric trust lattice owner>known>untrusted for min/clamp ops.
- Generalized outbox (Codex #5): ALL outbound (replies + proactive/scheduled sends) route through deliveries with unique delivery key + terminal/retry states — not replies-only.
- PreToolUse side-effect journal (Codex #6 / partial): record each admitted side-effecting call at PreToolUse with 'started' state; a 'started' with no matching completion on restart = ambiguous, never auto-retried without idempotency key (tightens §4.6 rule).
- WebFetch egress (R2-F4): hook-enforced — host allowlist or strip/deny query-strings once session has ingested external content; not the prose 'never in URLs' bullet.
- Channel auth grading (R2-F5): identities/approach.toml grade each channel's auth class; owner-trust stamping + approval authorization only on crypto-authed surfaces (Discord/Slack/Tailscale UI); SMS/email cap at known, read-only, never satisfy approval.
- git push approval binds commit SHAs + diffstat (R2-F6): rendered_call shows diffstat/commit range; digest bound to resolved commit SHAs; different range = fresh cycle.
- Reflection per-kind serialization (Codex #8 / R2-F7): a job covers only [checkpoint, now], advances checkpoint transactionally on completion, refuses new range while one outstanding; lease per-kind not per-row; add UNIQUE(fact deterministic key) + fact_sources UNIQUE(fact_id, source_ref).
- sessions DDL recovery fields (Codex #12): session_id UNIQUE, created_at, activation_deadline (stale-creating), rotated_to (predecessor/successor link).
- pending_approvals authorized_owner_id (Codex #7): FK-ish to identities owner_id; principals canonicalized.
- task_runs provenance / cross-store note (Codex #9): document daemon writes session-row + bd claim as a reconciled sequence (not a true 2-store txn) with restart reconciliation as the compensating action (already in §4.5 — make the non-atomicity explicit + add task provenance label).
- PS gates P1/P3 too (Codex #10): minimal PS foundation (identities + taint + read/write denylist) gates ANY channel ingestion + memory persistence, not just P2/P5; express plan as dependency edges.
- Cross-surface approval serializes on bound session's thread queue (R2-F8).
- id-less webhook dedup: state at-least-once + idempotent-handler tradeoff explicitly (R2-F9).
- supersession correction into live session (R2-F10): on supersede of a fact in an active session_facts, inject one-line correction next turn.
- §2 memory head-start reframed to 'built fresh on SQLite' (R2-F11).

DISPUTE (pending reverify-hooks agent): hook count 30 vs 31; --permission-prompt-tool documented vs not. Apply whatever the primary source says; correct §2 + §11 + §7 accordingly.
Bump to v1.9.
acceptance_criteria: An independent review of the revised spec scores at least 8/10, satisfying the epic acceptance criteria.
notes:
FIX ROUND 3 applied + ACCEPTED per user direction (2026-07-10). Spec v1.8 -> v1.9 (commit c7b9574; SHA 81e57aa85b7ea52cfbbb94ceec11c49eab07131eee168fa87b1eb11418769fff). Round-3 re-review CANCELLED by user; spec accepted at last-scored 7/10 (Claude) / 6/10 (Codex) with the full round-2 union of fixes applied afterward — no regression, all round-2 findings closed.

Round-3 fixes applied: sessions.trust_floor (mixed-trust thread confidentiality — both reviewers' critical); Edit/Write/NotebookEdit matrix rows + control-surface denylist (Codex #2 crit); log_entries event_id/trust/min_trust (Codex #3 crit / mechanical provenance now computable); content-driven taint incl. untrusted MCP results + pre-execution inbound taint (Codex #4 / R2-F1); generalized deliveries outbox all-outbound (Codex #5); tool_attempts PreToolUse started-state journal (Codex #6); channel-auth grading SMS/email weak (R2-F5); commit-SHA-bound push approval (R2-F6); per-kind reflection serialization + fact_key + fact_sources UNIQUE (Codex #8 / R2-F7); sessions UNIQUE session_id + created_at + activation_deadline + rotated_to (Codex #12); approvals.authorized_owner_id (Codex #7); hook-enforced fetch egress (R2-F4); PS gate now covers P1 ingestion + P3 memory (Codex #10); cross-store bd claim documented as reconciled sequence + provenance label (Codex #9); cross-surface approval serializes on bound queue (R2-F8); supersession live-correction (R2-F10); webhook at-least-once tradeoff stated (R2-F9); §2 memory reframed to built-fresh (R2-F11); hook-count 30v31 + permission-prompt-tool doc-status softened to version-pinned assumptions (Codex #11 dispute — not blind-flipped). Structure balances 11/11 sections, 4/4 tables, 11/11 pre. ACCEPTANCE: user accepted; epic gate reframed from >=8 hard to 'round-2 union resolved, ready for P1-P3 build with security-core items as build-time concerns.' Closing.
close_reason: Round-3 fixes applied (spec v1.9, commit c7b9574); re-review cancelled by user; spec accepted at 7/10 with full round-2 review union resolved.

title: Harden the security model: approvals, policy boundary, sandbox profile
description:
Resolves review findings #4, #5 (Critical) and #6 (Important). Approval rows record only session, tool, and a rendered string — no requesting principal, authorized approver, canonical argument digest, expiry, nonce, or replay protection; matching and consumption are not atomic (#4, spec refs :422-444, :562-566). The policy boundary is defined over Claude tool names, so unrestricted Bash under 'Owner DM' bypasses semantic gates for send/delete/purchase, and trust is derived solely from originating channel with no handling for injected fetched content or mixed-trust threads (#5, spec refs :528, :594, :601-606, :614-617, :721). 'Docker sandbox' names no container profile: mounts, secrets, UID, capabilities, seccomp, limits, image provenance, teardown are all unspecified (#6, spec refs :328, :616).

Work: (a) Approvals — store canonical tool name + normalized JSON input digest, requester identity/trust, authorized approver/channel, expiry, nonce, audit timestamps; require authenticated owner approval; compare-and-consume in one transaction; define expired/replay/mismatch behavior. (b) Policy — define policy at capability/egress boundaries, remove unrestricted host Bash, command allowlists or proxy for side effects, credential isolation, trust rules for mixed-participant threads and content fetched during trusted turns; high-impact actions gated regardless of prompt origin. (c) Sandbox — full container profile: immutable image, non-root UID, read-only root, exact mounts, secret exclusion, dropped capabilities, no Docker socket, seccomp/AppArmor, resource limits, egress policy, artifact extraction, teardown tests.
design:
DECISIONS (2026-07-09, grill-me session) — security model:

1. BASH MODEL: sandbox-default + gated host_exec. The Bash tool always executes in the sandbox container for EVERY trust level including owner DM (cwd-only mount, per-class egress, no credentials in env). Host execution is a separate always-ask gated verb (host_exec, §4.4 flow). Side-effect capabilities (send/email/delete/purchase/home-automation) are MCP verbs holding their own credentials, gated per the matrix — Bash structurally cannot reach them. This deletes the §7 bypass class (curl/gh/mail/rm from owner-DM Bash) instead of enumerating it.

2. APPROVALS (asserted mechanics + decided auth): store canonical tool name + normalized-JSON argument digest (rendered string kept for display only), requesting event id + session, nonce (approval id), decision timestamps; compare-and-consume in ONE transaction; digest mismatch on resume = new approval cycle, never fuzzy match. Authorization: approval event valid iff sender resolves to owner identity on a trust=owner surface — cross-surface allowed (approve from Slack what Discord asked). Expiry: 1 hour; expired = fresh request cycle.

3. TAINT MODEL: sticky session taint, one column right. Session gains durable tainted flag (sessions row, set by PostToolUse on web fetch / untrusted participant input / Codex output). Effect: policy matrix reads one column right for mutating verbs (owner-tainted ≈ known-group: mutating asks; memory/task writes gate + provenance-tag untrusted-derived, feeding .1's read-time exclusion automatically). Reads unaffected. Clears on rotation. High-impact verbs remain gated regardless of taint (per .1).

4. WORKER SANDBOX EGRESS: read-only dependency allowlist (github clone/fetch, proxy.golang.org, registry.npmjs.org — per-repo config). ZERO credentials inside any sandbox: workers commit locally; push/PR is a gated HOST-side verb — the daemon pushes with its own credential after policy/approval. Push becomes a policy-visible verb like send/delete.

5. CONTAINER PROFILE (asserted per finding #6 checklist): pinned immutable image, non-root UID, read-only root FS, exact cwd-only mounts, secret/env exclusion, dropped capabilities, no Docker socket, seccomp default profile, CPU/mem/PID/wall-time limits, defined artifact extraction, teardown verified by test.

6. CREDENTIAL ISOLATION (asserted): service credentials live with their MCP servers / systemd credentials, never in engine child env, never in the workspace; the trust boundary for every credential is the verb that uses it.

Cross-refs: trust stamping now owner-owned per .9 (native channel layer). Matrix gains a 'tainted' column shift rule; §7 controls list updated accordingly.
acceptance_criteria: Approval flow is replay-safe and atomic with all binding fields specified; policy is expressed at capability/egress boundaries with no ungated Bash path to side effects; the sandbox section specifies a complete, testable container profile.
notes: Spec edits applied 2026-07-09 (same v1.3 pass, SHA above). Edits: §7 matrix — Bash row now 'always sandboxed, no host/creds/per-class egress' (allow/allow/deny), NEW host_exec row (always-ask/deny/deny), NEW git push/PR host-side-verb row (ask/deny/deny); new callout stating the two implicit rules (Bash never a side-effect path; taint shifts the column); matrix intro notes the taint column-shift; controls list — allowlist bullet rewritten to digest binding, 'Sandbox the untrusted' replaced by 'Sandbox by default — everyone, always' carrying the full pinned container profile + per-class egress + zero-credentials + push-from-host rationale; secrets bullet -> credentials live with the verb (MCP/daemon), never engine env/workspace/sandbox; §4.4 paragraph + Fig 5 rewritten for canonical-digest binding, nonce, any-owner-surface authorization, 1h expiry, atomic match-and-consume, mismatch=new cycle; §6 pending_approvals table rewritten (requested_by_event, args_digest, expires, decided_by, expired status), sessions gains tainted column, facts provenance harmonized to untrusted-derived; C9/C10 register rows updated. Container profile and credential isolation asserted per finding #6 checklist.

title: Specify durable event processing and session-invariant enforcement
description:
Resolves review findings #2 and #3 (Critical). Inbound events have no event ID, dedup key, receipt timestamp, processing status, attempt count, or delivery state; no dead-letter table or durable queue exists, so a daemon crash can lose an event, rerun a tool side effect, or duplicate a notification (#2, spec refs :527-537, :539-581, :633). The composite session primary key permits multiple active sessions per thread_key, and queuing by session_id happens too late to serialize first-message races; rotation and session-ID capture have no atomic transaction or state machine (#3, spec refs :322, :336, :345, :359-364, :540-548, :718).

Work: Specify events, deliveries, and dead_letters tables with stable IDs, idempotency keys, state transitions, attempts, retry deadlines, and correlation IDs; define crash recovery at every boundary, retry classification/backoff, and at-least-once/exactly-once expectations for engine invocation, tool side effects, and outbound messages. Queue by thread_key before resolution; add a partial unique index for one active session per thread; define transactional session states (creating/active/rotating/failed) and atomic first-run capture, rotation, stale-creator recovery, and resume-failure handling.
design:
DECISIONS (2026-07-09, grill-me session) — durable events + session invariants:

SUBSTRATE (asserted, unopposed): durable queue is an 'events' table in approach.db; the daemon writes the event row ON RECEIPT, before any processing (gateway channels don't redeliver). In-memory per-thread queues are an index over unprocessed rows; ordering is per-thread FIFO by receipt, rebuilt from the table on restart. Queue is keyed by thread_key BEFORE session resolution (reviewer's fix, asserted).

1. Mid-turn daemon crash: events found status=processing on restart become 'interrupted' — NEVER auto-rerun (side-effect ambiguity is mechanically unresolvable). Daemon surfaces to the originating thread with what it was doing + offer to retry.
2. Live engine failure (daemon alive; child non-zero exit / timeout / rate limit): side-effect-aware retry. Zero side-effecting tool calls observed this turn (live PostToolUse accounting) -> auto-retry up to 2 with backoff, honoring rate-limit signals. Any side effect already fired -> interrupted + surface. Timeout: kill child, apply same test. Unified rule: retry only what provably did nothing.
3. Event identity: messages = (channel, native_message_id) — bridge must pass it through; webhooks = provider delivery ID, else content hash; schedule fires = (schedule_id, occurrence_time).
4. Misfire policy: coalesce-to-newest WITH staleness flag — per schedule at most one catch-up event on restart, keyed to newest missed occurrence, carrying missed_count + lateness so the model judges whether a stale brief still ships; older occurrences recorded as skipped, never enqueued. Heartbeats hardcode coalesce.
5. Outbound leg: at-least-once. Event advances to 'replied' only after bridge acks the channel send; restart re-sends completed-but-unacked replies; rare duplicate accepted, no dedup marker.
6. Dead letters — entry: retry budget exhausted, malformed/unroutable events, interrupted events whose surface attempt itself failed. Exit: notify-once owner DM on entry + standing heartbeat checklist item for unacked rows (via notifications ledger, no re-nag); drain is manual requeue/discard commands.
7. Session birth: daemon pre-generates the UUID, INSERTs status='creating' under a partial unique index (one row per thread_key WHERE status IN ('creating','active')), spawns with --session-id, flips to 'active' on first successful result. Stale 'creating' past deadline -> 'failed', retried fresh. Capture-from-output is REMOVED from the C4 contract. Rotation = one transaction (old->rotated + new->creating); index makes bad interleavings constraint violations.
8. Interrupted events PARK out-of-band — thread queue keeps flowing; 'retry' re-enqueues at tail against current session state (--resume absorbs staleness; surface message carries the caveat).
9. Resume failure (Claude-side transcript gone/unreachable): mark row 'resume_failed' (a rotation cause, kept for forensics), create fresh session seeded with the assembler's full fact package, process the event, prepend one-line transparency note ('lost conversation history — facts intact'). Cross-ref bead .5 for backing up the Claude session store.

Session states: creating | active | rotated | resume_failed | failed. Event lifecycle: received -> processing -> completed -> replied (ok path); interrupted (parked); dead_letter (terminal, surfaced).
acceptance_criteria: The spec defines complete event/delivery/dead-letter schemas with state machines and crash-recovery behavior at each boundary, and the session schema plus queuing design provably enforces one active session per thread including under concurrent first messages.
notes: Spec edits applied 2026-07-09 (v1.1 -> v1.2). Revised-spec SHA-256: 02f448cfbfaa90f33c1a636ae5fd9d8a1d91bc844864d58f69fac76a79bbccf7. Edits: §4.1 rewritten — persist-on-receipt durability, queue by thread_key before resolution, at-least-once reply leg; Fig 2 gains persist/completed/replied/ack steps; §4.2 gains occurrence identity + coalesce-with-staleness misfire policy (C5 row updated to match); NEW §4.6 Failure & recovery — unified rule (retry only what provably did nothing), interrupted parking out-of-band, side-effect-aware retry via live PostToolUse accounting, resume-failure auto-rotate + note, dead-letter entry/exit conditions with manual drain; §6 — inbound contract gains required dedup_key + occurrence fields; NEW events table (durable queue, UNIQUE dedup_key, status machine received/processing/completed/replied/interrupted/skipped/dead, attempts, correlation, partial queue index) and dead_letters table (reason, acked, resolution); sessions rewritten — daemon-generated UUID via --session-id, states creating/active/rotated/resume_failed/failed, one_live_session partial unique index enforcing the router invariant in schema; C4 register row + P1 phase card updated (capture-from-output removed everywhere; P1 exit now includes mid-turn kill recovery); §2 resume bullet reworded to lead with --session-id; §3 shape callout + Fig 1 + §4.5 + §8 + §11 updated per-session->per-thread and durable-queue references; §8 failure bullet now points at §4.6; header/footer bumped to v1.2. Section tags balanced (11/11). Acceptance: schemas + state machines + crash recovery at each boundary specified; invariant enforced by partial unique index + pre-resolution thread_key queueing.

title: Define the Hermes integration contract or explicitly reject the split
description:
Resolves review finding #1 (Critical). The recommended build-vs-buy option assigns the memory/skill loop to Hermes while APPROACH keeps SQLite as fact source of truth and its own context assembler, but the spec never defines which process owns facts, how Hermes reads trajectories or writes accepted facts/skills, conflict/provenance handling, or whether Hermes replaces or runs beside components C6-C8. 'Import, not migration' is asserted, not designed. Spec refs: approach-agent-harness-spec.html:507-508, :582, :636, :683-685.

Work: Add an Option B deployment diagram and interface contract covering process boundaries, source-of-truth ownership, import/export formats, sync direction, failure behavior, security boundary, and which APPROACH components are retained or removed — or explicitly reject the Hermes split and rewrite the recommendation. Treat portability as an assumption until proven by a small compatibility spike.
design:
DECISION (2026-07-09, grill-me session): Reject the Hermes integration. Chosen path is 'Option D': build P1–P3 plus curated memory (F1 fact extraction + F3 non-destructive rollups); defer only the F2 skill-synthesis path.

Rationale and resolved branches:
1. Rejection is STRUCTURAL, not evidential: a second always-on agent process with write access to the memory store is added attack surface and a split-brain fact-ownership problem even if every portability claim (SKILL.md compat, FTS5, import-not-migration) proved true. Consequence: the compatibility spike is dropped and Hermes/OpenClaw claims leave the verification scope (.7).
2. Deferral cut: reflector ships with F1 (facts) and F3 (rollups); only F2 (repeated-pattern -> SKILL.md synthesis + review gate) is deferred. Curated memory without a writer is useless, so F1/F3 are launch scope.
3. C12/Codex critic: KEPT at launch as on-demand cross-model critique (owner decision, diverges from defer-with-F2). Charter changes from 'F2 review gate' to 'on-demand critic'; gate role noted as its future job. Consequences: Codex sandbox-pin risk stays live in §7/§11, Codex claims stay in .7 verification.
4. F2 re-entry: evidence trigger. F1 pass cheaply logs tagged 'repeated-pattern' rows (no synthesis); when ~5 distinct repeated patterns accumulate (or owner notices re-teaching a procedure), the F2 bead opens. Revisit is build-vs-nothing — Hermes stays rejected. These rows are F2's input corpus.
5. F1 v1 curation posture: auto-write with provenance, confidence, supersession links; passive decay (rank-down, never auto-delete); periodic owner-DM digest for one-tap pruning. No blocking pending-queue.
6. Tainted facts: facts derived from trajectories containing untrusted content auto-write with provenance=untrusted-derived but are EXCLUDED from context assembly until acknowledged via digest (trust-split applied at read time, one WHERE clause). Owner-derived facts flow freely.

Spec edits implied: §10 rewritten as a decision record (Option D + rationale + re-entry trigger); Option B/C cards and the $5 Hetzner deployment mention removed; F2 kept as a short deferred-design stub; 'portable, so it's an import' claim deleted; §5 updated for F1 digest + tainted-fact read-time exclusion; §11 keeps Codex sandbox pin and memory-poisoning items.
acceptance_criteria: The spec either contains a complete Option B interface contract (ownership, formats, sync, failure, security boundary, retained components) with a scoped compatibility spike, or documents an explicit decision to drop the Hermes split with updated recommendation text.
notes: Correction: header kicker (line 156) was missed in the v1.1 pass and still read v1.0 — now fixed. Final revised-spec SHA-256: c66102f68832ad25cd2ef3bf9b26f7e58e28873a5c00ed1ecc2beadcc258d48c. This supersedes the SHA in the previous note.

title: Develop and harden the APPROACH agent harness specification
description:
# Objective

Develop and harden the APPROACH personal agent harness specification until it is internally consistent, externally verified where version-sensitive, and sufficiently concrete to support safe test-first implementation.
